tier_1_regimens.notes: Standard regimen for stage I–IV high-grade serous, endometrioid (grade 2/3), clear cell, and carcinosarcoma. For stage I disease: 6 cycles for high-grade serous; 3–6 cycles for all other types. For stage II–IV disease: 6 cycles recommended. Albumin-bound paclitaxel may be substituted for hypersensitivity to paclitaxel. Individuals >70 years or with comorbidities may require alternate dosing.

drugs.name: Bevacizumab
drugs.schedule: Day 1, every 21 days; continue up to 12 additional cycles after chemo

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage II–IV disease or previously untreated recurrent disease. Bevacizumab-containing regimens should be used with caution before surgery due to potential interference with postoperative healing. If used as part of neoadjuvant, withhold bevacizumab 4–6 weeks prior to IDS. Bevacizumab continued for up to 12 additional cycles (total including chemo up to 15 months).

drugs.name: Bevacizumab
drugs.schedule: Starting day 1 of cycle 2, every 21 days for up to 22 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage II–IV disease or previously untreated recurrent disease. Median PFS 10.3 months in chemotherapy group vs 14.1 months in bevacizumab-throughout group. No difference in OS between groups.
Similar PFS, OS, and global QOL scores as paclitaxel/carboplatin. May be useful for patients at high risk for neuropathy (eg, diabetes).
Higher response rate but similar PFS and OS as paclitaxel/carboplatin. May be useful for select patients at high risk for neurotoxicity or to avoid alopecia. Repeat every 28 days for 3–6 cycles.
6 cycles (18 weeks). May be considered for those with poor performance status. Albumin-bound paclitaxel may be substituted for hypersensitivity reactions.

For optimally debulked stage II–III disease. Repeat every 21 days for 5–6 cycles. Patients must have normal renal function. Prior to each cycle, adequate IV fluids needed to prevent renal toxicity. Published trial used IV continuous infusion paclitaxel over 24 hours.
Repeat every 21 days for 6–8 cycles. IP carboplatin as part of paclitaxel/carboplatin/bevacizumab combination did not improve PFS or OS compared to IV administration.
Cisplatin associated with higher rates of neurotoxicity, GI toxicities, renal toxicity, metabolic toxicities, anemia, and alopecia compared to carboplatin. Repeat every 21 days for 3–9 cycles.

drugs.name: Bevacizumab
drugs.schedule: Day 1, every 21 days; continue to complete 1 year of therapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: Repeat every 21 days for 6 cycles, then continue Bevacizumab to complete 1 year of therapy.
For individuals >70 years and/or those with comorbidities. 3–6 cycles. Algorithms have been developed for predicting chemotherapy toxicity.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Carboplatin weekly/Paclitaxel weekly (alternate dosing for elderly/comorbidities)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Repeat every 21 days for 6 cycles (18 weeks). For individuals >70 years and/or those with comorbidities.
GI-type chemotherapy for mucinous histology. Retrospective data suggest improved PFS (HR 0.4, 95% CI 0.1–0.97) and OS (HR 0.2, 95% CI 0.1–0.8) with GI regimens compared to gynecologic regimens.
For mucinous carcinoma of the ovary, stage II–IV disease. Alternative GI-type chemotherapy regimen.
For mucinous carcinoma stage II–IV disease. Albumin-bound paclitaxel may be substituted for hypersensitivity.
For mucinous carcinoma stage II–IV disease. Bevacizumab-containing regimen.

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Grade Serous Carcinoma / Grade 1 Endometrioid Carcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: For low-grade serous (stage IC)/Grade 1 endometrioid (stage IC) and stage II–IV. Maintenance letrozole is a category 2A recommendation for low-grade serous and category 2B for grade 1 endometrioid. Tamoxifen is NOT recommended for low-grade serous carcinoma.

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Grade Serous Carcinoma / Grade 1 Endometrioid Carcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: For low-grade serous (stage IC)/Grade 1 endometrioid (stage IC) and stage II–IV. Aromatase inhibitors are preferred hormonal therapy if not used previously.

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Grade Serous Carcinoma / Grade 1 Endometrioid Carcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: For low-grade serous (stage IC)/Grade 1 endometrioid (stage IC) and stage II–IV. Tamoxifen is NOT recommended for low-grade serous carcinoma. Preferred if aromatase inhibitor was given previously.

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Grade Serous Carcinoma / Grade 1 Endometrioid Carcinoma

drugs.name: Bevacizumab
drugs.schedule: Day 1, every 21 days; maintenance bevacizumab (ICON-7 & GOG-218)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For low-grade serous/Grade 1 endometrioid stage II–IV disease. ICON-7 & GOG-218 dosing.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)

biomarkers.requirement: Testing recommended if not previously done

tier_1_regimens.notes: Up to 6 cycles. Based on small case series studies. Consider early referral to stem cell transplant center for autologous stem cell collection at the time of cycle 1.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)
tier_1_regimens.notes: 4–6 cycles. Dose-intensive regimen based on prospective studies in SCCOHT.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)
tier_1_regimens.notes: 3–4 cycles. Repeat every 21 days. Recommend pulmonary function test if considering bleomycin.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)
tier_1_regimens.notes: 4 cycles. Based on small case series studies in SCCOHT.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on dramatic response to checkpoint inhibitor reported in case report.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Dual anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 blockade. Phase II basket trial of dual anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 blockade in rare tumors.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary (Hypercalcemic Type - SCCOHT)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on activity in relapsed epithelial ovarian cancer and absence of a taxane in the primary therapy regimens.

For select patients with stage II–III resected dysgerminoma for whom minimizing toxicity is critical. 3 cycles. Consultation with a pediatric oncologist for pediatric/adolescent patients is recommended.

Repeat every 21 days for 3 cycles for good risk (category 2B), or 4 cycles for poor risk. For any stage embryonal tumors, endodermal sinus tumors, stage II–IV dysgerminoma, stage I grade 2–3 or stage II–IV immature teratoma, and any stage nongestational choriocarcinoma. Recommend pulmonary function test if considering bleomycin.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: TIP (Paclitaxel/Ifosfamide/Mesna/Cisplatin) - Malignant Germ Cell Tumors Recurrence
tier_1_regimens.notes: Potentially curative regimen for recurrent malignant germ cell tumors. Patients should be referred to tertiary care institution for HCT consultation and potentially curative therapy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: High-dose chemotherapy + HCT - Malignant Germ Cell Tumors Recurrence
tier_1_regimens.notes: High-dose chemotherapy regimens vary among institutions. Some patients are potentially curable with HCT. Patients with potentially curable recurrent germ cell disease should be referred to a tertiary care institution for HCT consultation and potentially curative therapy.
Palliative only. For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors.
Palliative only. For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors.
Palliative only. For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors.
Palliative only. For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors.
Palliative only. For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors.
For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors. Useful in certain circumstances.
For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors. Useful in certain circumstances.
For recurrent malignant germ cell tumors. Useful in certain circumstances.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel - Sex Cord-Stromal Tumors Primary Therapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred regimen for stage II–IV or all others with intermediate risk (eg, heterologous elements). Acceptable options include carboplatin/paclitaxel (preferred), EP, or BEP (category 2B).
Acceptable option for adjuvant therapy of sex cord-stromal tumors (category 2B).
Acceptable option for adjuvant therapy of sex cord-stromal tumors (category 2B). Recommend pulmonary function test if considering bleomycin.
Preferred recurrence therapy for malignant sex cord-stromal tumors.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Targeted Therapy - Bevacizumab (Sex Cord-Stromal Tumors Recurrence)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single agent bevacizumab as targeted therapy for recurrent sex cord-stromal tumors. Useful in certain circumstances.

Combination platinum-based chemotherapy preferred for first recurrence (category 1). OCEANS trial showed improvement in PFS and response rates with addition of bevacizumab. Contraindicated for patients at increased risk of GI perforation. Bevacizumab can be continued as maintenance if response.
Equivalent to carboplatin/paclitaxel but different toxicity profile. May be easier to tolerate.

Combination platinum-based chemotherapy preferred for first recurrence (category 1). GOG-0213 showed bevacizumab slightly increased median OS when combined with chemotherapy (HR 0.829; 95% CI 0.683–1.005; P=.056). Albumin-bound paclitaxel may be substituted for confirmed taxane hypersensitivity.
Platinum-based combination chemotherapy for platinum-sensitive recurrence.
For patients who have been treated with up to three lines of therapy and prior bevacizumab. Steroid premedication should not be used. Phase 3 open-label, randomised, controlled trial.
Preferred cytotoxic therapy for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.
Preferred cytotoxic therapy for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.
Preferred cytotoxic therapy for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.

AURELIA trial showed bevacizumab combined with chemotherapy improved ORR and PFS compared to chemotherapy alone in platinum-resistant disease. Contraindicated for patients at increased risk of GI perforation.
Preferred cytotoxic therapy. Weekly schedule. Albumin-bound paclitaxel may be substituted for hypersensitivity.
Preferred regimen for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer. AURELIA trial data.
Metronomic oral cyclophosphamide with bevacizumab for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.
Preferred cytotoxic therapy for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.
Preferred regimen for platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.

For FRα-expressing tumors (≥75% positive tumor cells). Phase 3 trial showed significantly longer OS compared with chemotherapy (median 16.46 vs 12.75 months; HR for death 0.67; 95% CI 0.50–0.89; P=.005). For patients treated with two prior lines of platinum-based therapy.
For FRα-expressing tumors (≥25% positive tumor cells). Promising activity regardless of FRα expression level or prior bevacizumab treatment.

For HER2-positive tumors (IHC 3+ or 2+). Category 2A for platinum-resistant, category 2B for platinum-sensitive. FDA granted accelerated approval for adult patients with unresectable or metastatic HER2-positive solid tumors. ORR 63.6% in IHC 3+ and 36.8% in IHC 2+ tumors.
For BRAF V600E-positive tumors. NCI-MATCH trial showed ORR 38%, PFS 11.4 months. Clinical benefits observed in 6 patients with primary gynecologic cancer.
NTRK inhibitors for NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors. Larotrectinib ORR 79% (95% CI 72%–85%) with 16% CR. FDA approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors.
For RET fusion-positive tumors. Tumor agnostic basket trial showed ORR 43.9% (95% CI 28.5–60.3) in 18 of 41 patients. FDA accelerated approval.

MEK1/2 inhibitor. Randomized trial showed median PFS 13.0 months vs 7.2 months for chemotherapy (HR 0.48; 95% CI 0.36–0.64; P<.0001). ORR 26% vs 6% for chemotherapy. Most common grade 3/4 AEs: skin rash, anemia, hypertension, diarrhea, nausea, fatigue.
Category 2B. Median PFS 9.1 vs 10.6 months for physician's choice chemotherapy (HR 1.21; 95% CI 0.79–1.86; P=.807). Numerically superior ORR (16% vs 13%).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Avutometinib/Defactinib - KRAS-mutated Low-Grade Serous Carcinoma Recurrence
tier_1_regimens.notes: For KRAS-mutated tumors (preferred). Phase II trial showed ORR 44% in KRAS-mutant vs 17% in KRAS wild-type. Median PFS 22.0 months vs 12.8 months. Most frequent grade ≥3 AEs: elevated creatine phosphokinase, diarrhea, anemia.
Tested in platinum-sensitive disease treated with ≥2 lines of prior therapy. Found to have consistent efficacy with tolerable safety.
For dMMR/MSI-H recurrent or advanced tumors. ORR 44.1% across multiple tumor types. 3 of 7 ovarian cancer patients had partial response. FDA accelerated approval.

biomarkers.requirement: MSI-H or dMMR, or TMB-H ≥10 mutations/megabase

tier_1_regimens.notes: For MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors or TMB-H tumors ≥10 mutations/megabase. 15 patients with ovarian cancer included; 3 CR and 2 PR observed. FDA approved for tumor-agnostic indication.

For PD-L1 CPS ≥1 tumors. Tumors with CPS ≥1 are considered PD-L1 positive. Interim analysis showed pembrolizumab significantly improved PFS in PD-L1 CPS ≥1 population (8.3 vs 7.2 months; P=.0014) and OS (18.2 vs 14 months; P=.0053). Phase 3 randomized double-blind trial.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab - Platinum-Resistant Clear Cell Carcinoma
tier_1_regimens.site: Clear Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary
tier_1_regimens.notes: For clear cell carcinoma. Phase 2 non-randomized trial showed ORR 55%, median duration of response not reached. Grade 3/4 AEs in 35% of patients. Another randomized phase 2 trial showed ORR 26.7% for combination vs 14.2% for nivolumab alone.
GI-type chemotherapy for recurrent mucinous carcinoma. Category 2B for bevacizumab addition.
For low-grade serous carcinoma. Recommended if aromatase inhibitor was given previously.
Category 2B. ORR 18% in phase 2 trial. Grade 3 ALT/AST elevations in 8% of patients.
Platinum agents have limited activity when disease has demonstrated growth through a platinum-based regimen. Platinum rechallenge is generally not recommended in this setting.
Response rate for single-agent bevacizumab is about 20%. May cause hypertension, arterial thrombosis, or intestinal perforation. Contraindicated for patients at increased risk of GI perforation.
Category 2B. Phase 2 superiority trial of niraparib plus bevacizumab versus niraparib alone.

For patients with germline or somatic BRCA1/2 P/LP variants who achieve CR/PR after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Up to 2 years unless disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. 7-year follow-up showed 67% alive vs 46% with placebo. Patients who received bevacizumab during primary therapy excluded.

For patients with HRD-positive tumors treated with olaparib/bevacizumab vs bevacizumab monotherapy. Clinically meaningful OS benefit for HRD-positive (HR 0.62; 95% CI 0.45–0.85). Olaparib up to 2 years; bevacizumab up to 15 months total (including chemotherapy). If unable to tolerate olaparib, consider niraparib + bevacizumab.

drugs.name: Niraparib
drugs.schedule: Daily (or 200 mg for patients with baseline body weight <77 kg and/or platelet count <150,000/mm3)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For BRCA1/2-mutated patients. Up to 3 years unless disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. PRIMA trial showed significant PFS improvement with niraparib vs placebo in HRD (21.9 vs 10.4 months; P<.001) and ITT (13.8 vs 8.2 months). No OS difference observed.
For BRCA1/2-mutated patients. Up to 2 years unless disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. ATHENA-MONO trial demonstrated significant clinical benefit in HRD and overall ITT population.

drugs.name: Niraparib
drugs.schedule: Daily (or 200 mg for patients with baseline body weight <77 kg and/or platelet count <150,000/mm3)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients unable to tolerate olaparib. Niraparib up to 3 years; bevacizumab up to 15 months total. OVARIO phase II trial: 62% of all patients, 76% HRD, 47% HRP, 56% HR unknown remained progression-free at 18 months. 5-year follow-up: median OS 61.1 months overall; not reached in HRD subgroup.

drugs.name: Niraparib
drugs.schedule: Daily (or 200 mg for patients with baseline body weight <77 kg and/or platelet count <150,000/mm3)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For germline BRCA1/2-mutated platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer after response to platinum therapy. Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Caution when using maintenance PARPi >24 months. Limited data on prior PARPi use.

For germline or somatic BRCA1/2 P/LP variants with platinum-sensitive recurrent disease after response to platinum therapy. Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Limited data on prior PARPi use.

For germline or somatic BRCA1/2 P/LP variants with platinum-sensitive recurrent disease after response to platinum therapy. Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. 21% of rucaparib patients had PFS ≥2 years vs 2% placebo.

biomarkers.requirement: If known, consider maintenance therapy post-primary therapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred adjuvant treatment for carcinosarcoma (MMMTs) of the ovary. If known BRCA1/2 mutation, consider maintenance therapy post-primary therapy. If not previously done, consider germline and somatic biomarker testing.
For carcinosarcoma. Useful in certain circumstances.
For carcinosarcoma. Useful in certain circumstances.
Category 2B for carcinosarcoma. Albumin-bound paclitaxel may be substituted for hypersensitivity.

tier_1_regimens.site: Clear Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary
tier_1_regimens.notes: For clear cell carcinoma recurrence. Randomized phase III trial compared irinotecan/cisplatin vs paclitaxel/carboplatin as first-line chemotherapy for ovarian clear cell carcinoma.
Single-agent cyclophosphamide for recurrent disease. Other recommended option.
Single-agent doxorubicin for recurrent disease. Other recommended option.
For recurrent platinum-sensitive disease. Other recommended option.
Single-agent paclitaxel for recurrent disease. Other recommended option.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel (single agent) - Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent albumin-bound paclitaxel for recurrent disease.
For recurrent platinum-sensitive disease. Other recommended option.
For recurrent platinum-sensitive disease. Other recommended option.
Prolonged oral etoposide as second-line therapy for platinum-resistant and platinum-sensitive ovarian carcinoma.
For recurrent platinum-resistant disease. Other recommended option.
For recurrent platinum-sensitive disease. Other recommended option.
For recurrent platinum-resistant disease. Phase II study showed combination is active.
Category 2B. For those previously treated with taxanes.

Multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Ovarian cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the ovary, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneum. The ovary is an organ of female reproductive system located in the pelvic cavity, with functions in gamete production and hormone secretion. Embryologically, the ovarian surface epithelium (germinal epithelium) is derived from the coelomic mesoderm, which also lines the fallopian tubes and peritoneum, explaining the common histological features and shared disease entities of ovarian, fallopian tube, and primary peritoneal cancers. The primary anatomical boundary of the ovary is its capsule; invasion beyond this capsule defines staging. Ovarian cancer is often diagnosed at an advanced stage due to the lack of specific early symptoms, with malignancy characterized by peritoneal spread (carcinomatosis), lymphatic metastasis, and hematogenous dissemination. The clinical presentation includes a suspicious/palpable pelvic mass, ascites, abdominal distention, and nonspecific symptoms like bloating, pelvic/abdomen pain, difficulty eating, and urinary symptoms.

subtypes.name: High-Grade Serous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: The most common and aggressive histologic subtype, typically arising from serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas (STICs) in the fallopian tube fimbriae. Characterized by papillary, solid, or slit-like glandular structures with marked nuclear atypia and frequent mitoses.
subtypes.frequency: Most common type of epithelial ovarian cancer.
subtypes.key_associations: Strong association with BRCA1/2 germline mutations and homologous recombination deficiency (HRD). TP53 mutations are nearly universal (>96%).

subtypes.name: Low-Grade Serous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A distinct, less aggressive entity from high-grade serous carcinoma. Arises from borderline serous tumors. Shows uniform, mild nuclear atypia with few mitoses. Often multifocal and bilateral.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for <5% of epithelial ovarian cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Frequently associated with activating mutations in the MAPK pathway (KRAS, BRAF). TP53 mutations are rare. Often diagnosed at a younger age.

subtypes.name: Endometrioid Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Resembles endometrial adenocarcinoma. Forms glandular structures similar to endometrium. Can be associated with endometriosis. Graded as grade 1, 2, or 3.
subtypes.frequency: Less common than serous, but accounts for 10-15% of epithelial cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Often associated with endometriosis. Microsatellite instability (MSI) and defects in mismatch repair (MMR) are more common than in other subtypes.

subtypes.name: Clear Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by cells with clear cytoplasm (due to glycogen) and hobnail cells. Often forms tubulocystic, papillary, or solid patterns. Frequently arises in endometriotic cysts.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for 5-10% of epithelial ovarian cancers; more common in Asian populations.
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with endometriosis. Often negative for WT1 and estrogen receptors. May have a better prognosis in early stages but is relatively chemoresistant in advanced stages.

subtypes.name: Mucinous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Tumors with abundant intracellular mucin. Often large, unilateral, and cystic. Histologically, can show expansile or infiltrative growth patterns. Must be distinguished from metastatic mucinous tumors from gastrointestinal tract.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 3% of epithelial ovarian cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Bilateral involvement is uncommon. Features favoring primary ovarian vs. metastatic include size >13 cm, smooth external surface, and absence of surface involvement.

subtypes.name: Carcinosarcoma (Malignant Mixed Müllerian Tumor)
subtypes.description: A rare, aggressive biphasic tumor composed of malignant epithelial (carcinoma) and mesenchymal (sarcoma) components.
subtypes.key_associations: Poor prognosis. Most are high-grade. Treatment is similar to high-grade serous carcinoma.

subtypes.name: Small Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary, Hypercalcemic Type (SCCOHT)
subtypes.description: A highly aggressive, rare tumor predominantly affecting young women. Characterized by small round blue cells and frequent paraneoplastic hypercalcemia.
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with germline and somatic mutations in the SMARCA4 (BRG1) gene.

subtypes.name: Malignant Germ Cell Tumors
subtypes.description: A group of tumors arising from primordial germ cells. Includes dysgerminoma, endodermal sinus tumor (yolk sac tumor), embryonal carcinoma, and immature teratoma. Often diagnosed in children and young adults.
subtypes.frequency: Predominant ovarian tumor type in children/adolescents; relatively rare in adults.
subtypes.key_associations: Elevated tumor markers: alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) for yolk sac tumor, beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (β-hCG) for choriocarcinoma/seminomatous elements. Often associated with gonadal dysgenesis.

subtypes.name: Malignant Sex Cord-Stromal Tumors
subtypes.description: A heterogeneous group of tumors derived from ovarian sex cords (granulosa cells, Sertoli cells) and stroma (theca cells, fibroblasts). Includes adult granulosa cell tumor and Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor.
subtypes.key_associations: Often estrogenic, leading to endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma. Inhibin and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) can be used as tumor markers for granulosa cell tumors.

subtypes.name: Borderline Epithelial Tumors (Low Malignant Potential)
subtypes.description: Tumors with cytologic atypia and epithelial proliferation but without frank stromal invasion. Typically have a clinically indolent course. Can have peritoneal implants (noninvasive or invasive).
subtypes.key_associations: Patients are often younger and present with early-stage disease. Five-year survival exceeds 80%. Noninvasive implants confer an increased risk of progression to low-grade serous carcinoma.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of ovarian cancer is highly subtype-dependent. High-grade serous carcinoma is characterized by near-universal TP53 mutations (>96%), and frequently exhibits homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) due to germline or somatic BRCA1/2 mutations or other alterations in HR genes (e.g., BRIP1, RAD51C/D). This genomic instability is a key therapeutic target for PARP inhibitors. In contrast, low-grade serous carcinomas are driven by activating mutations in the MAPK pathway, with BRAF V600E mutations found in approximately 30-50% and KRAS mutations in 20-30% of cases. TP53 mutations are rare in this subtype. Endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas are often associated with endometriosis and have distinct molecular profiles. Endometrioid carcinomas frequently harbor CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutations, ARID1A mutations, and microsatellite instability (MSI) due to defects in mismatch repair. Clear cell carcinomas commonly show ARID1A mutations and PIK3CA mutations. Mucinous carcinomas often have KRAS mutations. Malignant germ cell tumors have distinct pathogenesis, with dysgerminomas associated with overexpression of KIT. Sex cord-stromal tumors, such as adult granulosa cell tumors, are characterized by a pathognomonic somatic FOXL2 C134W mutation. Small cell carcinoma of the ovary, hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT) is defined by bi-allelic loss-of-function mutations in the SMARCA4 (BRG1) gene.

epidemiology.incidence: In 2026, it is estimated that 21,010 new diagnoses of ovarian cancer will occur in the United States [1]. Ovarian cancer is the sixth most common cause of cancer mortality in females in the United States.
epidemiology.mortality: It is estimated that 12,450 deaths from ovarian cancer will occur in the United States in 2026 [1]. Five-year survival is about 52% overall, but is longer for select patients with early-stage disease and certain histologic subtypes [2,3].
epidemiology.trends: Trends of ovarian cancer incidence by histotype and race/ethnicity have been analyzed [3].
epidemiology.demographics: Age-adjusted incidence is highest in older women. Most patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage. Risk factors include inherited genetic mutations (BRCA1/2), older age, and reproductive history. Incidence varies by histologic subtype and race/ethnicity [3].

risk_factors.detail: Carriers of confirmed BRCA1/2 pathogenic/likely pathogenic (P/LP) variants are at significantly increased risk. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) is effective in reducing risk [6,7,8,9].
Not having been pregnant increases risk. Conversely, each pregnancy/birth confers a 30% to 60% decreased risk [10,11,12].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to Strong. Supported by multiple epidemiological studies.

risk_factors.detail: Breastfeeding is associated with a decreased risk of ovarian cancer [10,12].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate. Consistent evidence of protective effect.

risk_factors.detail: Use of oral contraceptives is associated with a decreased risk of ovarian cancer [10,12].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to Strong. Consistent evidence of protective effect.

risk_factors.detail: Data suggest that postmenopausal hormone therapy may increase the risk, although results vary across studies [12,14,15,16].
risk_factors.strength: Variable/Moderate. Evidence is inconsistent across studies.

risk_factors.detail: The risk for ovarian borderline epithelial tumors may be increased after ovarian stimulation for in vitro fertilization [17,18].
risk_factors.strength: Weak to Moderate. Evidence is inconsistent.

risk_factors.detail: Obesity has been shown to be associated with increased risk, but the data are inconsistent [12,13,19,20].
Smoking has been shown to be associated with increased risk of some histologic subtypes, but the data are inconsistent overall [12,13,21,22].
risk_factors.strength: Weak to Moderate. Inconsistent data, primarily associated with mucinous histology.

protective_factors: Pregnancy and childbirth (nulliparity is a risk factor)

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Ovarian cancer, including fallopian tube and primary peritoneal cancer, most commonly presents with a constellation of nonspecific abdominal and pelvic symptoms. The classic presentation includes bloating, pelvic or abdominal pain, difficulty eating or feeling full quickly (early satiety), and urinary symptoms such as urgency or frequency. These symptoms are especially concerning when they are new, occur more than 12 times per month, and cannot be attributed to any known or previously identified cause [23,24]. On physical examination, a suspicious or palpable pelvic mass may be identified during abdomen/pelvis exam, and ascites or abdominal distention may be present. The fifth most common cause of cancer mortality in females in the United States, epithelial ovarian cancer accounts for an estimated 21,010 new diagnoses and 12,450 deaths in the United States in 2026 [1]. Five-year survival is approximately 52%, although survival is longer for select patients with early-stage disease and certain histologic subtypes [2,3]. Less common ovarian cancers (LCOCs) include carcinosarcoma, clear cell carcinoma, mucinous carcinoma, low-grade serous carcinoma, small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT), grade 1 endometrioid carcinoma, borderline epithelial tumors, malignant sex cord-stromal tumors, and malignant germ cell tumors. Mucinous tumors typically present as very large cystic masses filling the abdomen and pelvis, often at a younger age (20–40 years), and are frequently diagnosed at early stage with a 5-year disease-free survival of approximately 80% to 90% [50,323]. Malignant germ cell tumors mainly occur in younger individuals with a median age at diagnosis of 16 to 20 years and are the predominant ovarian tumor in this age group [362–365]. Low-grade serous carcinoma represents less than 5% of epithelial ovarian cancers and presents at a younger age than high-grade serous carcinoma, but is often at an advanced stage by diagnosis [334–338].

symptoms.name: Bloating
symptoms.detail: Persistent or progressive abdominal bloating is one of the most common presenting symptoms of ovarian cancer. When bloating is new, occurs more than 12 times per month, and cannot be attributed to a known cause, it should raise suspicion for ovarian malignancy [23,24].
symptoms.frequency: Common; among the four cardinal symptoms of ovarian cancer

symptoms.name: Pelvic or abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Pelvic or abdominal pain is a frequent presenting complaint. Pain may be vague, persistent, and non-menstrual in character. It may be constant or intermittent and is often described as a dull ache or pressure sensation.
symptoms.frequency: Common; among the four cardinal symptoms of ovarian cancer

symptoms.name: Difficulty eating or feeling full quickly (early satiety)
symptoms.detail: Patients may report early satiety, decreased appetite, or difficulty eating. This symptom often leads to changes in dietary habits and can contribute to poor nutritional status, which is associated with higher risk of suboptimal surgery, surgical complications, and poor survival, especially in patients ≥75 years [47–49].
symptoms.frequency: Common; among the four cardinal symptoms of ovarian cancer

symptoms.name: Urinary symptoms (urgency or frequency)
symptoms.detail: Urinary urgency or frequency may result from direct compression of the bladder by the ovarian mass or from peritoneal spread of disease.
symptoms.frequency: Common; among the four cardinal symptoms of ovarian cancer

symptoms.name: Abdominal distention / ascites
symptoms.detail: Clinically apparent ascites and abdominal distention may be present at diagnosis, particularly in advanced-stage disease. Ascites can be diagnosed on physical examination and confirmed with imaging.

symptoms.name: Palpable pelvic mass
symptoms.detail: A suspicious or palpable mass may be identified on abdomen/pelvis examination. This finding warrants urgent further evaluation with imaging and tumor marker testing.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss may be a presenting symptom and can indicate advanced or disseminated disease.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; may indicate advanced disease

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Fatigue may result from anemia, cachexia, or the systemic effects of advanced malignancy.

symptoms.name: Menstrual irregularities
symptoms.detail: Abnormal vaginal bleeding may occur, particularly with sex cord-stromal tumors or endometrioid tumors that may involve the endometrium.

symptoms.name: Symptoms of hypercalcemia (SCCOHT)
symptoms.detail: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT) is specifically associated with hypercalcemia, which can present with nausea, vomiting, polyuria, polydipsia, confusion, and constipation [329].

symptoms.name: Hormonal symptoms (sex cord-stromal tumors)
symptoms.detail: Granulosa cell tumors and other sex cord-stromal tumors may produce estrogen or androgens, leading to symptoms such as abnormal uterine bleeding, endometrial hyperplasia, virilization, or precocious puberty in children.
symptoms.frequency: Variable; dependent on tumor hormone production

signs.name: Palpable pelvic/abdominal mass
signs.detail: A solid or complex adnexal or pelvic mass may be palpable on bimanual pelvic examination or abdominal palpation. Masses that are complex, solid, or have irregular borders are more likely to be malignant.

signs.name: Ascites
signs.detail: Abdominal distention with shifting dullness and fluid wave on percussion suggests ascites, which is a common finding in advanced ovarian cancer. Paracentesis may be performed for cytologic evaluation.
signs.frequency: Common, especially in stage III–IV disease

signs.name: Abdominal distention
signs.detail: Visible abdominal distention may be due to ascites, large tumor burden, or bowel obstruction.

signs.name: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable Virchow node (left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy) may indicate metastatic spread and is an ominous finding.

signs.name: Pelvic tenderness or fullness
signs.detail: Pelvic examination may reveal tenderness, fixation, or a feeling of fullness suggestive of a pelvic mass or peritoneal disease.

atypical_or_missed: Symptoms of ovarian cancer are often vague, nonspecific, and overlap with common gastrointestinal and genitourinary conditions, leading to frequent misattribution and delayed diagnosis. The classic constellation of bloating, pelvic pain, early satiety, and urinary symptoms is often attributed to irritable bowel syndrome, menopause, or aging, resulting in an average delay of 3–6 months from symptom onset to diagnosis [23,24].

Early-stage disease is often asymptomatic and is frequently discovered incidentally during physical examination, imaging for other indications, or at the time of surgery for a presumed benign condition.
Mucinous tumors may grow to very large sizes before detection and can be misidentified as pseudomyxoma peritonei from an appendiceal or gastrointestinal primary.

Germ cell tumors in adolescents and young adults may present with acute abdominal pain mimicking appendicitis or ectopic pregnancy, or may be found during evaluation of amenorrhea or precocious puberty.
Sex cord-stromal tumors may present with hormonal manifestations (estrogenic or androgenic effects) that may be attributed to other causes before the ovarian mass is identified.
Borderline ovarian tumors (low malignant potential) often present at a younger age with early-stage disease and may be discovered during workup for infertility.

Occult serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas (STICs), considered precursors of high-grade serous carcinoma, may be discovered incidentally during risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy and present management challenges [53].
Primary peritoneal and fallopian tube cancers are usually diagnosed postoperatively and may not have a visible ovarian mass on imaging, leading to diagnostic uncertainty.

red_flags: New onset of persistent bloating, pelvic/abdominal pain, early satiety, and urinary symptoms occurring more than 12 times per month that cannot be attributed to a known cause should prompt urgent evaluation for ovarian cancer [23,24].
Palpable pelvic or abdominal mass on examination requires urgent imaging workup and referral to a gynecologic oncologist.
Ascites or abdominal distention without an obvious non-malignant cause warrants urgent investigation.
CA-125 elevation in the setting of a pelvic mass or symptoms suggestive of ovarian cancer requires expedited workup.
Rising CA-125 after completion of primary therapy for ovarian cancer may indicate recurrence and requires imaging and clinical evaluation.

Symptoms suggestive of bowel obstruction (severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, inability to pass flatus or stool) in a patient with known or suspected ovarian cancer represent a surgical emergency.
Hypercalcemia in a young woman with an ovarian mass is characteristic of small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT) and requires urgent evaluation [329].
Acute abdomen in a young patient with an ovarian mass may indicate torsion, rupture, or hemorrhage and requires urgent surgical evaluation.
Rapidly enlarging pelvic mass or new-onset ascites in a patient with known borderline tumor may indicate transformation to invasive carcinoma.

Any patient with suspected ovarian malignancy should be referred promptly to a gynecologic oncologist, as primary assessment and debulking by a gynecologic oncologist has been shown to result in a survival advantage [51].

diagnostic.rationale: Ultrasound is typically the first-line imaging modality for evaluating adnexal masses and can effectively triage the majority of adnexal masses into benign or malignant categories. It is the most commonly used initial imaging study [25,26].
diagnostic.key_findings: Features suggesting malignancy include solid components, complex cystic and solid architecture, thick septations, papillary projections, increased vascularity on Doppler, and ascites. However, ultrasound alone has limited sensitivity for staging.

diagnostic.test: Abdomen/pelvis (A/P) CT with oral and IV contrast
diagnostic.rationale: A/P CT is recommended for preoperative staging assessment, estimation of resectability, and surgical planning. It is generally more useful than ultrasound for assessing abdominal metastases [28,31]. CT should be performed with oral and iodinated IV contrast (unless contraindicated due to anaphylaxis or significant renal dysfunction) with or without rectal contrast.
diagnostic.key_findings: Peritoneal implants, omental cake, lymphadenopathy, liver or splenic lesions, hydronephrosis, and ascites. Chest CT can detect metastatic or disseminated disease.

diagnostic.rationale: MRI has been shown to provide equivalent accuracy for staging and comparable accuracy for predicting peritoneal tumor volume compared with CT. It can be useful if CT results are inconclusive and is preferred in select patients with renal dysfunction over CT [27,28]. MRI is performed with gadolinium-based contrast agents (unless contraindicated due to anaphylaxis).
diagnostic.key_findings: MRI provides superior soft tissue characterization of adnexal masses, helps differentiate benign from malignant lesions, and can identify peritoneal disease extent.

diagnostic.rationale: FDG-PET/CT for assessing advanced disease may be useful if CT results are indeterminate and may be able to detect distant metastases [32,33]. The Panel recommends PET/CT, MRI, or PET/MRI for indeterminate lesions if the results of the scan will alter management. PET/CT is performed from skull base to mid-thigh.
diagnostic.key_findings: Metabolic activity in peritoneal implants, lymph nodes, or distant metastases. May detect disease not visible on conventional imaging.

diagnostic.test: Chest CT with or without IV contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Although there is no direct evidence that chest CT is necessary, the Panel recommends it as part of the overall evaluation of a patient if clinically indicated. Chest CT can detect metastatic or disseminated disease, which may help with treatment planning.
diagnostic.key_findings: Pulmonary metastases, pleural effusions, mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

diagnostic.rationale: Intraoperative pathologic evaluation with frozen sections may assist in surgical management decisions, particularly for determining whether fertility-sparing surgery is appropriate and for guiding the extent of surgical staging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic subtype and grade of tumor, which influence the extent of surgery and adjuvant therapy decisions.

diagnostic.rationale: When biopsy is not feasible, cytopathology from ascites or pleural effusion combined with a CA-125:CEA ratio of >25 can be used to support the diagnosis of ovarian cancer.
diagnostic.key_findings: Malignant cells in peritoneal fluid are diagnostic of peritoneal spread.

diagnostic.rationale: FNA should be avoided for diagnosis of ovarian cancer in patients with presumed early-stage disease to prevent rupturing the cyst and spilling malignant cells into the peritoneal cavity. However, FNA may be necessary in patients with bulky disease who are not candidates for primary debulking [2,3].

GI tract evaluation is especially important in those with elevated serum CEA and an adnexal mass to distinguish between a GI primary tumor that has metastasized to the ovary and primary mucinous carcinoma of the ovary. Upper and lower GI tract should be carefully evaluated for mucinous histology [50].
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification or exclusion of a GI primary malignancy.

diagnostic.rationale: Refer patients who wish to retain fertility to a reproductive endocrinologist for consultation, including possibility of oocyte collection before chemotherapy is initiated.
diagnostic.key_findings: Assessment of fertility potential and counseling regarding fertility-sparing surgical options.

staging.rationale: Baseline CBC is essential to assess for anemia, thrombocytopenia, or leukopenia that may affect treatment planning and to establish baseline values for monitoring during chemotherapy.
staging.indication: All patients at initial workup and prior to each cycle of chemotherapy.

staging.test: Comprehensive metabolic panel (chemistry profile) with liver function tests (LFTs)
staging.rationale: Assessment of renal and hepatic function is required to determine eligibility for platinum-based chemotherapy and to monitor for treatment-related toxicities.
staging.indication: All patients at initial workup and prior to each cycle of chemotherapy.

staging.rationale: Serum CA-125 levels tend to correlate with the clinical course of disease, especially in those with elevated pretreatment levels. CA-125 can be useful in treatment planning, monitoring response to therapy, and surveillance for recurrence [34,35]. The CA-125:CEA ratio of >25 (combined with cytopathology from ascites or pleural effusion) may be suspicious for ovarian cancer when biopsy is not feasible.
staging.indication: All patients with clinically suspicious disease, and for monitoring during and after treatment if initially elevated.

staging.rationale: Additional tumor markers may include inhibin, beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (β-hCG), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), CA 19-9, and HE4 [38–42]. HE4 may be a useful prognostic marker and may improve early detection of recurrence relative to CA-125 alone [35–37].
staging.indication: Inhibin, AFP, β-hCG, LDH for germ cell and sex cord-stromal tumors. CEA and CA 19-9 for mucinous tumors. AFP should be measured in individuals <35 years with a pelvic mass to assess for germ cell tumors and rule out pregnancy [292–294].

staging.rationale: Comprehensive cross-sectional imaging from chest to pelvis is used for staging assessment. C/A/P CT is the standard imaging modality for staging and follow-up of epithelial ovarian cancer, fallopian tube cancer, and primary peritoneal cancer [OV-A].
staging.indication: All patients with suspected ovarian malignancy, and for monitoring/follow-up of stage I–IV disease not receiving treatment.

staging.rationale: Chest CT and A/P MRI may be used as an alternative to C/A/P CT for staging assessment, particularly when CT is inconclusive or when MRI provides superior soft tissue characterization [OV-A].
staging.indication: Patients receiving platinum-based chemotherapy for stage II–IV disease, and for monitoring/follow-up.

staging.test: PET/MRI or PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh)
staging.rationale: PET imaging may be useful for detection of recurrent disease, evaluation of indeterminate lesions, and assessment of disease extent. PET/CT or PET/MRI is recommended for patients with recurrent disease [OV-A].
staging.indication: Recurrent disease; indeterminate lesions if results will alter management.

staging.rationale: In select patients with advanced-stage disease, laparoscopy may be useful to evaluate whether optimal cytoreduction can be achieved by primary debulking surgery or whether neoadjuvant chemotherapy may be a better initial treatment option [72–75]. A randomized trial showed that only 10% of patients randomized to assessment laparoscopy had >1 cm residual disease after surgery compared to 39% who underwent primary cytoreductive surgery [73].
staging.indication: Select patients with advanced-stage or recurrent disease to determine feasibility of optimal cytoreduction.

biomarkers.rationale: Germline and somatic BRCA1/2 pathogenic/likely pathogenic (P/LP) variants inform maintenance therapy selection with PARP inhibitors. All patients with suspected ovarian cancer should have genetic risk evaluation and germline and somatic BRCA1/2 testing if not previously done [c,d,e,h].
biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis or during primary treatment, prior to initiation of maintenance therapy.

biomarkers.rationale: In the absence of a BRCA1/2 mutation, HRD status may provide information on the magnitude of benefit of PARP inhibitor (PARPi) therapy. Current clinical HRD tests are proxy measures of HRD and lack accuracy in fully predicting functional HRD. HRD testing is recommended for those patients without germline BRCA1/2 P/LP variants [OV-C].
biomarkers.when: In patients without BRCA1/2 P/LP variants, at the time of upfront testing or at recurrence.

biomarkers.test: Tumor biomarker testing (comprehensive) for recurrence setting
biomarkers.rationale: Validated biomarker testing should be performed in a CLIA-approved facility using the most recent available tumor tissue. Tumor biomarker analysis is recommended to include tests to identify potential benefit from targeted therapeutics that have tumor-specific or tumor-agnostic benefit including, but not limited to: HER2 status (by IHC), PD-L1 (IHC, CPS), BRCA1/2, HRD status, microsatellite instability (MSI), mismatch repair (MMR), tumor mutational burden (TMB), BRAF, KRAS, FRα (FOLR1), RET, and NTRK1/2/3 [OV-C, OV-6, OV-7, OV-8].
biomarkers.when: At recurrence or persistent disease, if not previously done. Multigene panel testing (MGPT) may be particularly important in LCOC with limited approved therapeutic options.

biomarkers.rationale: PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS) testing is relevant for selection of immunotherapy, specifically pembrolizumab. Tumors with a CPS ≥1 determined by an FDA-approved test are considered PD-L1 positive [footnote bb].
biomarkers.when: At recurrence when considering immunotherapy, particularly for platinum-resistant disease.

biomarkers.rationale: MSI-H and dMMR tumors may respond to checkpoint inhibitor therapy (dostarlimab-gxly, pembrolizumab) with tumor-agnostic FDA approval [42,43].
FRα expression is relevant for selection of mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx, an antibody-drug conjugate with activity in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer [35,36,53,54,274,277].
biomarkers.when: At recurrence when considering mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx.

biomarkers.rationale: TMB-H (≥10 mutations/megabase) may identify patients who could benefit from pembrolizumab based on tumor-agnostic indication [43].

Germline SMARCA4 testing should be performed after surgery if not previously done in patients with small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type. If germline SMARCA4 negative, fertility-sparing surgery can be considered after appropriate counseling, though there are limited data on the safety and feasibility of this approach.
MSI/MMR testing is recommended for all patients with endometrioid carcinoma, as it may identify patients who could benefit from immunotherapy and has implications for Lynch syndrome screening.

do_not_miss: BRCA1/2 testing (germline and somatic) — essential for maintenance therapy selection and genetic risk evaluation for the patient and family members.
Tumor biomarker testing at recurrence — to identify eligibility for targeted therapies including PARPi, immunotherapy, antibody-drug conjugates, and tumor-agnostic agents.
CA-125 measurement at baseline and during follow-up — for monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence.

Gynecologic oncology evaluation — all patients with suspected ovarian malignancy should be referred to a gynecologic oncologist, as primary assessment and debulking by a gynecologic oncologist results in a survival advantage [51].
Comprehensive surgical staging — approximately 30% of patients with presumed early-stage disease are upstaged after complete surgical staging [66–69].
GI evaluation for mucinous histology — to rule out occult GI primary with ovarian metastases.
AFP measurement in patients <35 years with a pelvic mass — to assess for germ cell tumors and rule out pregnancy.
Genetic risk evaluation — all patients with ovarian cancer should have genetic risk evaluation per NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment [c,d,e].
MSI/MMR testing for all endometrioid carcinomas — to identify Lynch syndrome and potential immunotherapy benefit.
avoid: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for diagnosis in patients with presumed early-stage disease — to prevent rupturing the cyst and spilling malignant cells into the peritoneal cavity.
Whole abdominal radiation therapy (RT) — rarely used for epithelial ovarian, primary peritoneal, and fallopian tube cancers at NCCN Member Institutions because of low efficacy and high toxicity.

Carboplatin monotherapy as first-line adjuvant chemotherapy for patients ≥70 years and/or those with comorbidities — based on the termination of the GINECO/GCIG trial, which showed worse survival outcomes associated with carboplatin monotherapy [192].

PARP inhibitor monotherapy as recurrence therapy — the FDA has requested voluntary restriction of second-line maintenance PARP inhibitor indications to patients with BRCA-mutated cancers; PARPi monotherapy has been removed as an option for recurrent disease [289,290].
Bevacizumab and PARP inhibitor combination as maintenance for recurrent disease — the Panel does not recommend this combination in the recurrence setting.

Systematic lymphadenectomy of clinically negative nodes — a randomized trial showed that lymphadenectomy in patients with stage IIB–IV ovarian cancer with macroscopically complete resection and normal nodes did not improve PFS or OS and was associated with increased rates of serious postoperative complications [133].

staging.system: AJCC 8th edition (2017) TNM and FIGO Staging System for Ovarian, Fallopian Tube, and Primary Peritoneal Cancer [ST-1, ST-2, ST-3]

t_stages.description: Tumor limited to ovaries (one or both) or fallopian tube(s).
Tumor limited to one ovary (capsule intact) or fallopian tube, no tumor on ovarian or fallopian tube surface; no malignant cells in ascites or peritoneal washings.
Tumor limited to both ovaries (capsules intact) or fallopian tubes; no tumor on ovarian or fallopian tube surface; no malignant cells in ascites or peritoneal washings.

Tumor limited to one or both ovaries or fallopian tubes, with any of the following: T1c1 (IC1) Surgical spill; T1c2 (IC2) Capsule ruptured before surgery or tumor on ovarian or fallopian tube surface; T1c3 (IC3) Malignant cells in ascites or peritoneal washings.
Tumor involves one or both ovaries or fallopian tubes with pelvic extension below pelvic brim or primary peritoneal cancer.
Extension and/or implants on the uterus and/or fallopian tube(s) and/or ovaries.
Extension to and/or implants on other pelvic tissues.

Tumor involves one or both ovaries or fallopian tubes, or primary peritoneal cancer, with microscopically confirmed peritoneal metastasis outside the pelvis and/or metastasis to the retroperitoneal (pelvic and/or para-aortic) lymph nodes.
Microscopic extrapelvic (above the pelvic brim) peritoneal involvement with or without positive retroperitoneal lymph nodes.
Macroscopic peritoneal metastasis beyond pelvis 2 cm or less in greatest dimension with or without metastasis to the retroperitoneal lymph nodes.

Macroscopic peritoneal metastasis beyond the pelvis more than 2 cm in greatest dimension with or without metastasis to the retroperitoneal lymph nodes (includes extension of tumor to capsule of liver and spleen without parenchymal involvement of either organ).

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Isolated tumor cells in regional lymph node(s) no greater than 0.2 mm.
Positive retroperitoneal lymph nodes only (histologically confirmed).
Metastasis up to and including 10 mm in greatest dimension.
Metastasis more than 10 mm in greatest dimension.

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis, including pleural effusion with positive cytology; liver or splenic parenchymal metastasis; metastasis to extra-abdominal organs (including inguinal lymph nodes and lymph nodes outside the abdominal cavity); and transmural involvement of intestine.
Liver or splenic parenchymal metastases; metastases to extra-abdominal organs (including inguinal lymph nodes and lymph nodes outside the abdominal cavity); transmural involvement of intestine.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to ovaries or fallopian tubes. Approximately 30% of patients with presumed early-stage disease are upstaged after comprehensive surgical staging [66–69]. Five-year survival is favorable for early-stage disease.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Generally >90% for stage IA; variable for IC based on histology and grade
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; surgery with comprehensive staging; observation or adjuvant therapy depending on histology, grade, and risk factors

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to one ovary (capsule intact) or fallopian tube, no tumor on surface, no malignant cells in ascites or washings.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; observation or adjuvant chemotherapy depending on histology and grade

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to both ovaries (capsules intact) or fallopian tubes, no tumor on surface, no malignant cells in ascites or washings.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; observation or adjuvant chemotherapy depending on histology and grade

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to ovaries or fallopian tubes with surgical spill, capsule rupture, tumor on surface, or malignant cells in ascites/washings.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy recommended for high-grade histologies

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor involves ovaries or fallopian tubes with pelvic extension below the pelvic brim.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Extension and/or implants on the uterus and/or fallopian tubes and/or ovaries.
Extension to and/or implants on other pelvic tissues.

stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Microscopic extrapelvic peritoneal involvement with or without positive retroperitoneal lymph nodes.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Macroscopic peritoneal metastasis beyond pelvis ≤2 cm with or without retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy; consider HIPEC with IDS

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Macroscopic peritoneal metastasis beyond pelvis >2 cm with or without retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis (includes extension to capsule of liver and spleen without parenchymal involvement).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy; consider HIPEC with IDS

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastasis including pleural effusion with positive cytology, liver/splenic parenchymal metastases, extra-abdominal metastases, or transmural intestinal involvement.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent with systemic therapy; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy; consider HIPEC for suitable patients (category 2B)
Curative intent with systemic therapy; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Liver or splenic parenchymal metastases, extra-abdominal metastases, or transmural intestinal involvement.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent with systemic therapy for responsive disease; platinum-based chemotherapy with or without bevacizumab; maintenance therapy; palliative care as appropriate

staging_pearls: Approximately 30% of patients with presumed early-stage disease are upstaged after undergoing comprehensive surgical staging, underscoring the importance of thorough staging procedures [66–69].

Data show that comprehensive surgical staging does not affect overall survival (OS) but is important for determining the most appropriate postoperative management options, including selection of adjuvant and maintenance therapy.

The distinction between platinum-sensitive and platinum-resistant disease represents a spectrum, and clinical judgment and flexibility should be utilized in determining treatment options. Platinum-sensitive disease is defined as relapse ≥6 months after completing prior chemotherapy; platinum-resistant disease is defined as relapse <6 months [ee].
Pathologists recommend categorizing serous ovarian cancer as either low-grade or high-grade; grade 2 serous is considered high-grade [q].

Approximately 30% of serous borderline tumors are associated with peritoneal implants, which may be either noninvasive or invasive. Invasive implants are synonymous with low-grade serous carcinoma and require adjuvant therapy. Noninvasive implants appear to confer at least a 15% to 20% increased risk of subsequent low-grade serous carcinoma [OV-C].

Post primary treatment recommendations (OV-5) for stage II–IV high-grade serous or grade 2 or 3 endometrioid carcinoma include consideration for maintenance therapy with PARP inhibitors and/or bevacizumab based on BRCA1/2 status and HRD status.

Data are limited for maintenance therapy with PARPi for patients with stage II disease, but the Panel decided that maintenance options recommended for stage III–IV disease should also be considered for stage II disease [x].

For less common ovarian cancers (LCOC), including carcinosarcoma, clear cell, mucinous, low-grade serous, grade 1 endometrioid, borderline epithelial, malignant sex cord-stromal tumors, and germ cell tumors, treatment recommendations follow specific algorithm pages (LCOC-1 through LCOC-15).
Germ cell tumors are the predominant ovarian tumor in adolescents and young adults (median age 16–20 years) and are often diagnosed at stage I with excellent prognosis [362–365].
SCCOHT is associated with SMARCA4 gene mutations and has molecular and genetic similarities to malignant rhabdoid tumors [329].

management_principles.overview: The management of ovarian, fallopian tube, and primary peritoneal cancer requires a multimodal approach, including surgery, systemic therapy, and sometimes radiation therapy. Treatment is individualized based on disease stage, histologic subtype, molecular biomarkers, and patient factors. A gynecologic oncologist should be involved in the assessment and treatment of all patients. Clinical trial participation is encouraged when applicable.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with newly diagnosed ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer, particularly those with early-stage disease or advanced disease amenable to complete cytoreduction.
by_intent.approach: Primary cytoreductive surgery (PCS) followed by adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy, often combined with bevacizumab or followed by PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy for patients with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) or BRCA mutations. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) is an alternative for patients with bulky disease unlikely to be optimally resected upfront.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with recurrent or persistent disease not amenable to curative-intent therapy.
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy), hormonal therapy, and/or palliative radiation therapy to control symptoms, improve quality of life, and prolong survival. Secondary cytoreductive surgery may be considered for select patients with platinum-sensitive, isolated recurrent disease.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients who have undergone primary surgery and have residual microscopic or macroscopic disease.
by_intent.approach: Postoperative systemic therapy to reduce the risk of recurrence and treat residual disease. The choice of regimen depends on stage, histology, and biomarker status.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with advanced-stage disease (e.g., stage IIIC/IV) who are poor surgical candidates or unlikely to achieve optimal cytoreduction with primary surgery.
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab) given prior to surgery to reduce tumor burden and improve the likelihood of successful interval cytoreduction.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: All patients with suspected ovarian malignancies should be evaluated by a gynecologic oncologist. A multidisciplinary team (MDT) including gynecologic oncologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and palliative care specialists is recommended for comprehensive treatment planning, especially for complex cases, recurrent disease, or less common histologic subtypes.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is used to assess a patient's ability to tolerate treatment. ECOG PS ≥2 may indicate poorer tolerance to aggressive surgery or combination chemotherapy. PS should be considered when determining eligibility for secondary cytoreductive surgery and systemic therapy regimens. Older adults (≥70 years) and those with comorbidities may require modified dosing or alternative regimens due to increased risk of toxicity.

management_pathways.title: Newly Diagnosed Stage IA or IB Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (Fertility Not Desired)

nodes.criteria: Stage IA or IB, high-grade serous or endometrioid (grade 2/3) carcinoma, no residual disease after surgery.

options.label: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging including hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), omentectomy, lymph node dissection, and peritoneal biopsies.

options.label: Observation or adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy
options.preference: Observation for low-risk patients; chemotherapy for high-risk features
options.recommendation: Observe for patients with stage IA grade 1 tumors. For stage IA grade 2/3 or stage IB, consider adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel for 3-6 cycles.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy may be offered for high-grade histology or stage IC disease. Observation is appropriate for stage IA grade 1 tumors.

management_pathways.title: Newly Diagnosed Stage II-IV Epithelial Ovarian Cancer

nodes.criteria: Surgical candidate, optimal cytoreduction likely (fertility not desired).

options.label: Primary cytoreductive surgery (PCS)
options.recommendation: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging and maximal cytoreductive effort. Goal is complete resection (R0) or optimal residual disease (<1 cm).

options.label: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel every 3 weeks for 6 cycles. Consider addition of bevacizumab (GOG-218/ICON-7 regimen) for high-risk disease.

options.label: PARP inhibitor or bevacizumab maintenance
options.recommendation: For patients with germline/somatic BRCA1/2 mutation or HRD: olaparib, niraparib, or rucaparib. If bevacizumab used, consider olaparib+bevacizumab or niraparib+bevacizumab. If no BRCA/HRD: bevacizumab maintenance or observe.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy is standard. Maintenance therapy with PARP inhibitors or bevacizumab may be added based on biomarkers and prior treatment.

nodes.criteria: Poor surgical candidate or low likelihood of optimal cytoreduction.

options.label: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT)
options.recommendation: Platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab for 3-4 cycles before reassessment for interval debulking surgery (IDS).

options.label: Interval debulking surgery (IDS)
options.recommendation: Hysterectomy/BSO with maximal cytoreduction. Consider HIPEC/cisplatin for stage III disease.

options.label: Adjuvant chemotherapy after IDS
options.recommendation: Complete a total of 6 cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy. May consider IP/IV regimen for optimally debulked patients.

adjuvant_logic.summary: NACT followed by IDS and adjuvant chemotherapy is an alternative to PCS. Maintenance therapy as above.

nodes.criteria: Relapse ≥6 months after completing prior platinum-based chemotherapy, isolated or limited foci of disease, good performance status, no ascites.

options.label: Secondary cytoreductive surgery
options.recommendation: Consider for patients with isolated or limited recurrent disease amenable to complete resection. May be combined with platinum-based chemotherapy.

options.label: Combination platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/gemcitabine ± bevacizumab, carboplatin/liposomal doxorubicin ± bevacizumab, or carboplatin/paclitaxel ± bevacizumab.

options.label: PARP inhibitor maintenance
options.recommendation: Olaparib, niraparib, or rucaparib for patients with germline/somatic BRCA1/2 mutation who have not progressed during prior PARP inhibitor therapy. Caution with use beyond 24 months due to MDS/AML risk.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Platinum-based rechallenge is standard. Maintenance with PARP inhibitors for BRCA-mutated tumors. Bevacizumab may be continued as maintenance.

nodes.criteria: Relapse ≥6 months after completing prior platinum-based chemotherapy, diffuse disease or not a surgical candidate.

options.label: Platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab
options.recommendation: As above. Alternatively, non-platinum regimens or clinical trials.

nodes.criteria: Progression on primary, maintenance, or recurrence therapy; or complete remission and relapse <6 months after completing platinum-based chemotherapy.

options.label: Non-platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Albumin-bound paclitaxel/relacorilant, docetaxel, gemcitabine, liposomal doxorubicin ± bevacizumab, weekly paclitaxel ± bevacizumab, topotecan ± bevacizumab, oral cyclophosphamide + bevacizumab.

options.label: Mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx
options.recommendation: Monotherapy for tumors with ≥75% positive tumor cells. Combination with bevacizumab for tumors with ≥50% positive tumor cells.

options.label: Pembrolizumab ± bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Weekly paclitaxel + pembrolizumab ± bevacizumab for tumors with CPS ≥1. Also, dostarlimab-gxly or pembrolizumab for dMMR/MSI-H tumors.

options.label: Ipilimumab + nivolumab
options.recommendation: For patients with recurrent clear-cell carcinoma.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Non-platinum-based systemic therapy is standard. Platinum rechallenge is generally not recommended.

options.label: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging. Fertility-sparing surgery not recommended.

options.label: Adjuvant IV carboplatin/paclitaxel
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel every 3 weeks for 3-6 cycles.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy is standard. Maintenance therapy with PARP inhibitor if BRCA mutation.

nodes.criteria: Clear cell carcinoma, stage IA-IC1 or IB, expansile pattern.

options.label: Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (USO) or BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Fertility-sparing surgery may be considered for select patients with stage IA disease.

options.label: Observation or adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy
options.preference: Observation for stage IA; chemotherapy for IC2-IC3 or higher
options.recommendation: Observe for stage IA/IB with expansile pattern. For IC2-IC3 or higher, carboplatin/paclitaxel ± bevacizumab.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation for early-stage low-risk tumors. Adjuvant chemotherapy for advanced or high-risk tumors.

options.label: USO or BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: GI evaluation to rule out metastasis. Appendectomy only if abnormal.

options.label: Observation or adjuvant chemotherapy
options.preference: Observation for stage IA expansile; chemotherapy for infiltrative or higher stage
options.recommendation: Observe for stage IA expansile carcinoma. For infiltrative or higher stage, consider GI-type chemotherapy (fluorouracil/leucovorin/oxaliplatin ± bevacizumab) or carboplatin/paclitaxel.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation for select early-stage tumors. Adjuvant chemotherapy for higher risk features.

nodes.criteria: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary, hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT), any stage.

options.label: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging. Germline SMARCA4 testing. Fertility-sparing surgery only if SMARCA4 negative.

options.label: Adjuvant chemotherapy
options.recommendation: VPCBAE (vinblastine/cisplatin/cyclophosphamide/bleomycin/doxorubicin/etoposide) for up to 6 cycles. Consider high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell transplant consolidation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Intensive adjuvant chemotherapy is standard. Clinical trials preferred.

nodes.criteria: Low-grade serous carcinoma, stage IC or higher.

options.label: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging. Fertility-sparing surgery for select early-stage patients.

options.label: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy ± hormonal therapy
options.preference: Chemotherapy for stage II-IV; observation or hormone therapy for stage IC
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel ± maintenance letrozole or other hormonal therapy. Hormone therapy (aromatase inhibitors) as alternative.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Platinum-based chemotherapy for advanced disease. Maintenance hormone therapy may be considered.

nodes.criteria: Grade 1 endometrioid carcinoma, stage IC or higher.

options.label: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging. Fertility-sparing surgery for select early-stage patients.

options.label: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy ± hormonal therapy
options.preference: Chemotherapy for stage II-IV; observation or hormone therapy for stage IC
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel ± maintenance letrozole or other hormonal therapy. Hormone therapy as alternative.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Platinum-based chemotherapy for advanced disease. Maintenance hormone therapy may be considered.

nodes.criteria: Ovarian serous borderline epithelial tumors (LMP), stage II-IV with noninvasive implants.

options.label: Cytoreductive surgery
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging and resection of residual disease. Lymph node evaluation may be considered case-by-case.

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Chemotherapy not beneficial for LMP tumors. Extended follow-up required due to risk of recurrence ≥5 years.

nodes.criteria: Malignant sex cord-stromal tumors, stage I high-risk or stage II-IV.

options.label: Hysterectomy/BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Complete surgical staging. Fertility-sparing surgery for stage I low-risk with complete staging.

options.label: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy
options.preference: Preferred for high-risk or advanced disease
options.recommendation: Carboplatin/paclitaxel (preferred), etoposide/cisplatin, or BEP (category 2B).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy for high-risk stage I or advanced disease.

nodes.criteria: Malignant germ cell tumors, any stage.

options.label: Fertility-sparing surgery or BSO with comprehensive staging
options.recommendation: Fertility-sparing surgery with comprehensive staging. Complete staging if fertility not desired. Pediatric patients may omit staging.

options.label: Adjuvant BEP
options.preference: Preferred for non-dysgerminoma histologies or stage II-IV
options.recommendation: BEP (bleomycin/etoposide/cisplatin) for 3-4 cycles. Carboplatin/etoposide for select dysgerminoma patients.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy with BEP for most histologies. Observation for stage I dysgerminoma or grade 1 immature teratoma.

items.text: Abdomen/pelvis exam for suspicious/palpable mass or ascites
items.recommendation: All patients with symptoms or signs of ovarian cancer.

items.text: Imaging studies as clinically indicated: pelvis ultrasound, A/P CT/MRI, chest CT. PET/CT, MRI, or PET/MRI for indeterminate lesions if results will alter management.
Complete blood count (CBC), chemistry profile with liver function tests (LFTs)
CA-125 or other tumor markers (inhibin, β-hCG, AFP, LDH, CEA, CA 19-9, HE4) as clinically indicated
items.recommendation: CA-125 for epithelial ovarian cancer; other markers for germ cell or sex cord-stromal tumors.
To rule out GI primary for mucinous tumors.

items.text: Reproductive endocrinology and infertility (REI) evaluation as clinically indicated

items.recommendation: All patients to assess for hereditary cancer syndromes.

items.text: Refer to gynecologic oncologist for clinically suspicious lesions
items.recommendation: All patients with suspected ovarian malignancy.

items.text: Histologic confirmation via biopsy or surgical specimen
items.recommendation: Preferred over FNA for presumed early-stage disease to avoid spillage.

items.text: Genetic risk evaluation and germline/somatic biomarker testing (BRCA1/2, HRD status)
items.recommendation: All patients with invasive epithelial ovarian cancer to inform maintenance therapy.

items.text: Tumor biomarker testing for recurrent disease (HER2, PD-L1, BRCA, HRD, MSI, MMR, TMB, BRAF, KRAS, FRα, RET, NTRK)
items.recommendation: If not previously done, to guide targeted therapy options.
To determine eligibility for surgery and systemic therapy.
Important for surgical planning and prognosis.

surgery.role: Primary treatment for ovarian cancer includes surgical staging and cytoreduction. Surgery is also used for interval debulking after neoadjuvant chemotherapy and for secondary cytoreduction in recurrent disease.
principles: An open laparotomy with vertical midline incision is standard for most patients. Minimally invasive techniques may be used by experienced surgeons for early-stage disease or interval debulking.
Goal is maximal cytoreduction: complete resection (R0) or optimal residual disease (<1 cm).
Comprehensive staging includes peritoneal cytology, peritoneal biopsies, omentectomy, para-aortic and pelvic lymph node dissection, and resection of all visible disease.
Fertility-sparing surgery (USO with uterus preservation) may be considered for select patients with apparent early-stage disease and low-risk histologies who desire fertility.
Special considerations for mucinous tumors (GI evaluation), borderline tumors (consider omentectomy and peritoneal biopsies), and LCOC (individualized approach).

procedures.name: Primary Cytoreductive Surgery (PCS)
procedures.approach: Open laparotomy; minimally invasive for select early-stage or interval debulking.
procedures.indications: Newly diagnosed ovarian cancer when optimal cytoreduction is feasible.
procedures.key_complications: Bowel resection, splenectomy, diaphragm stripping, hemorrhage, infection, urinary tract injury.
procedures.operative_mortality: Low (<1%) in high-volume centers; higher with extensive upper abdominal surgery.

procedures.name: Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS)
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
procedures.indications: Patients with advanced disease who received NACT and achieved response or stable disease.
procedures.key_complications: Similar to PCS; may consider HIPEC with cisplatin for stage III disease.

procedures.name: Secondary Cytoreductive Surgery
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive for isolated recurrent disease.
procedures.indications: Platinum-sensitive recurrence (≥6 months), good performance status, no ascites, resectable disease.
procedures.key_complications: Bowel resection, adhesiolysis, risk of fistula.

procedures.name: Risk-Reducing Salpingo-Oophorectomy (RRSO)
procedures.indications: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers for cancer prevention.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Optimal cytoreduction is defined as residual disease <1 cm. Complete resection (R0) is the goal. Factors affecting resectability include disease distribution, patient performance status, and surgeon experience. Laparoscopic assessment may predict ability to achieve optimal cytoreduction.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Assess nutritional status, performance status, and comorbidities. Correct anemia, electrolyte imbalances. Consider ERAS protocols to reduce length of stay. Counsel patients about potential for IP chemotherapy and need for port placement.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is not a standard primary treatment for epithelial ovarian cancer due to low efficacy and high toxicity. It may be used for palliation of symptoms or for oligometastatic disease in recurrent settings.
principles: Whole abdominal RT is rarely used at NCCN Member Institutions.
