Localized RT can be considered to palliate symptoms (e.g., bone metastases, brain metastases) or for oligometastatic disease.
RT is not part of primary adjuvant treatment for epithelial ovarian cancer.

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT for bone metastases
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or 8 Gy in 1 fraction

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT for brain metastases
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or 20 Gy in 5 fractions

approaches.name: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Varies by indication; typically palliative dosing.
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, myelosuppression depending on field.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is a cornerstone of ovarian cancer treatment, used in adjuvant, neoadjuvant, and recurrent settings. Platinum-based chemotherapy is standard. Targeted therapies (PARP inhibitors, bevacizumab) and immunotherapies are used based on biomarkers. Treatment is tailored by stage, histology, and molecular profile.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant therapy for stage I disease (high-grade serous, endometrioid grade 2/3)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum-based chemotherapy reduces recurrence risk in high-risk early-stage disease.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant therapy for stage II-IV disease
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Carboplatin/paclitaxel ± bevacizumab (GOG-218/ICON-7 regimen) every 3 weeks for 6 cycles
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum-based chemotherapy is standard. Bevacizumab adds benefit in high-risk disease.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Weekly paclitaxel/weekly carboplatin, carboplatin/docetaxel, carboplatin/liposomal doxorubicin, IP/IV regimens
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specific for chemotherapy. Bevacizumab may be added regardless of biomarkers.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: NACT reduces tumor burden to improve surgical outcomes in poor surgical candidates.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Maintenance therapy post primary treatment (stage II-IV)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Based on BRCA/HRD status and prior bevacizumab use. For BRCA-mutated: olaparib, niraparib, or rucaparib. For BRCA wild-type/HRD-positive: olaparib+bevacizumab or niraparib+bevacizumab if bevacizumab used during primary therapy.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: PARP inhibitors improve PFS and OS in BRCA-mutated and HRD-positive tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Bevacizumab monotherapy if no BRCA/HRD mutation, or observation.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRCA1/2 germline/somatic testing and HRD status testing.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Combination platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab (e.g., carboplatin/gemcitabine ± bevacizumab)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum-based rechallenge is standard for sensitive disease.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Carboplatin/liposomal doxorubicin ± bevacizumab, carboplatin/paclitaxel ± bevacizumab, cisplatin/gemcitabine
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specific for chemotherapy. PARP inhibitor maintenance for BRCA-mutated tumors.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Non-platinum-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab (e.g., weekly paclitaxel ± bevacizumab, liposomal doxorubicin ± bevacizumab) or mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx for FRα-expressing tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Platinum rechallenge generally not recommended. Non-platinum agents are standard.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Docetaxel, gemcitabine, topotecan ± bevacizumab, oral cyclophosphamide + bevacizumab
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 (CPS ≥1) for pembrolizumab combination; FRα expression for mirvetuximab; MSI-H/dMMR for immunotherapy.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel every 3 weeks
key_regimens.setting: Primary therapy for stage I-IV disease
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, neuropathy, alopecia, nausea, fatigue, hypersensitivity reactions (platinum).

key_regimens.name: GOG-218 regimen (Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab)
key_regimens.setting: Primary therapy for stage II-IV disease
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 21 days for 6 cycles; continue bevacizumab for up to 22 cycles total.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, proteinuria, hemorrhage, GI perforation, wound healing complications.

key_regimens.name: ICON-7 regimen (Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab)
key_regimens.setting: Primary therapy for stage II-IV disease
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 21 days for 6 cycles; continue bevacizumab for up to 12 additional cycles.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Gemcitabine + Bevacizumab (OCEANS)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 21 days for 6-10 cycles; continue bevacizumab as maintenance.

key_regimens.name: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Relacorilant
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 21 days. Steroid premedication should NOT be used.

key_regimens.name: BEP (Bleomycin/Etoposide/Cisplatin)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuropathy, bleomycin pulmonary toxicity.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Response to Treatment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: During and after completion of systemic therapy, with imaging as clinically indicated. CA-125 levels are monitored if initially elevated.
response_logic: Complete Response (CR): No definitive evidence of disease on imaging and normalization of tumor markers.
Partial Response (PR): Decrease in disease burden on imaging and/or decrease in tumor markers.
Stable Disease (SD): No significant change in disease burden.
Progressive Disease (PD): Increase in disease burden on imaging and/or rising tumor markers.
imaging_recommendations: Patients receiving primary chemotherapy: C/A/P CT every 1-3 cycles as clinically indicated.
Patients receiving platinum-based chemotherapy for stage II-IV: Chest CT & A/P MRI or PET/MRI/PET/CT as clinically indicated.
Monitoring/Follow-up for patients not receiving treatment: C/A/P CT or Chest CT & A/P MRI as clinically indicated.
Recurrent disease: PET/MRI or PET/CT as clinically indicated.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Tissue biopsy may be considered for uncertain lesions or to confirm recurrence.
Secondary cytoreductive surgery may be considered for isolated recurrent platinum-sensitive disease.
Treatment for persistent disease or recurrence depends on platinum sensitivity, prior therapies, and biomarker status.

prognosis.overall: Ovarian cancer, including fallopian tube and primary peritoneal cancers, has a five-year overall survival rate of approximately 52% in the United States [1]. However, prognosis varies significantly by stage, histologic subtype, and molecular profile. Early-stage disease (stage I) is associated with a favorable prognosis, while advanced-stage disease (stage III-IV) has a poorer outcome due to peritoneal dissemination and challenges in achieving complete cytoreduction [2,3].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Approximately 90% (estimated from general data; specific percentages not explicitly stated in source)
by_stage.context: Localized to ovaries or fallopian tubes; prognosis is excellent with comprehensive staging and adjuvant therapy for high-risk histologies [2,3].
Pelvic extension; prognosis is intermediate, and treatment typically includes surgery followed by adjuvant chemotherapy [2,3].
Peritoneal metastasis outside the pelvis; prognosis is poor, with treatment focused on cytoreductive surgery and platinum-based chemotherapy with or without targeted therapies [2,3].
Distant metastasis; prognosis is very poor, with systemic therapy as the mainstay, including chemotherapy, bevacizumab, and maintenance PARP inhibitors [2,3].

prognostic_factors: Stage at diagnosis: Higher stage correlates with worse prognosis [2].
Histologic subtype: High-grade serous carcinoma is more aggressive, while low-grade serous and borderline tumors have better outcomes [1,2].
Residual disease after surgery: Optimal cytoreduction (residual disease <1 cm) is associated with improved survival [102-108].
Molecular markers: BRCA1/2 mutations and homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) status predict response to PARP inhibitors, affecting survival [207-214].
Performance status: Poor performance status (ECOG ≥2) is linked to shorter survival and reduced tolerance to therapy [45,46].
Tumor grade: Higher grade correlates with more aggressive disease and worse prognosis [2].
CA-125 levels: Elevated pre-treatment CA-125 may indicate advanced disease and poorer outcomes [34,35].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in ovarian cancer is multifactorial, with stage being the most critical determinant. Molecular profiling, particularly BRCA mutations and HRD status, increasingly guides maintenance therapy and predicts benefit from PARP inhibitors. Surgical outcome is pivotal, as complete resection is associated with improved survival. Additionally, histologic subtypes like clear cell and mucinous carcinomas may have different prognostic trajectories and treatment responses compared to high-grade serous carcinoma [1,2,207-214].

disease_related.management: Paracentesis for symptom relief; consider indwelling peritoneal catheter for recurrent ascites [OV-B 5 of 5].
Supportive care with IV fluids, electrolyte management; surgical intervention for mechanical obstruction [OV-B 5 of 5].
Prophylactic anticoagulation as per clinical guidelines; therapeutic anticoagulation for established VTE.
Multimodal approach including analgesics, nerve blocks, and palliative radiation for localized pain [OV-6, Palliative Care].

chemotherapy: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia): Monitor CBC, use growth factors if indicated [OV-D].
Neurotoxicity (peripheral neuropathy): Dose modifications for platinum/taxane agents; consider alternative agents like pegylated liposomal doxorubicin [OV-D].
Nephrotoxicity: Hydration with cisplatin; monitor renal function [OV-D].
Hypersensitivity reactions to platinum agents: Managed with desensitization protocols or switching agents [OV-E].
Alopecia: Consider scalp cooling for prevention [OV-D].
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs): Monitor for pneumonitis, colitis, endocrinopathies; manage with corticosteroids [OV-D].
Infusion reactions: Premedication and slower infusion rates [OV-E].
targeted_therapy: Bevacizumab: Hypertension, proteinuria, GI perforation risk; monitor blood pressure and urine protein [OV-D].
PARP inhibitors: Myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea; monitor blood counts and adjust dose [OV-D].
Mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx: Ocular toxicity, peripheral neuropathy; monitor vision and neurological symptoms [OV-D].
surgery: Surgical site infections: Antibiotic prophylaxis and wound care [OV-B].
Lymphedema: Lymph node dissection-related; manage with compression and physical therapy [OV-B].
Bowel complications: Ileus, anastomotic leak; supportive care and potential reoperation [OV-B].
radiation: Acute toxicities: Fatigue, dermatitis, gastrointestinal symptoms [OV-D].
Late effects: Fibrosis, secondary malignancies; long-term monitoring [OV-D].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral throughout ovarian cancer management, focusing on symptom control, quality of life, and management of treatment-related toxicities. It should be initiated early and integrated with oncologic care [NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Evaluate nutritional status preoperatively and during treatment. Poor nutritional status is associated with suboptimal surgery and worse outcomes [47-49]. Provide dietary counseling, nutritional supplements, and consider parenteral nutrition if needed [OV-1].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Follow antiemetic guidelines based on emetogenic potential of chemotherapy regimens. For high emetogenic regimens (e.g., cisplatin), use a combination of 5-HT3 antagonists, NK1 antagonists, and dexamethasone. For moderate emetogenic regimens, use 5-HT3 antagonists and dexamethasone [OV-D].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Use granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) for primary prophylaxis in regimens with high febrile neutropenia risk (>20%) or secondary prophylaxis after febrile neutropenia episode. Dose-dense regimens may require G-CSF support [OV-D].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Consider pharmacologic prophylaxis (e.g., low-molecular-weight heparin) for high-risk patients, including those with advanced disease, surgery, or immobilization. Mechanical prophylaxis with sequential compression devices perioperatively [OV-B].
supportive_care.pain_management: Assess pain regularly using validated scales. Use WHO analgesic ladder: non-opioids for mild pain, weak opioids for moderate pain, strong opioids for severe pain. Adjuvant agents for neuropathic pain [NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Refer to psychosocial services for distress management, anxiety, depression, and social determinants of health. Use the Distress Thermometer and Problem List [OV-4, footnote t].
supportive_care.dental_care: Oral examination before chemotherapy, especially with regimens causing mucositis. Encourage good oral hygiene and manage xerostomia [NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After completion of primary treatment, patients enter a surveillance phase with regular clinical visits, imaging, and laboratory monitoring. The schedule is more intensive initially and decreases over time [OV-6].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Surveillance aims to detect recurrence early when it may be amenable to secondary cytoreduction or systemic therapy. Rising CA-125 or symptoms should prompt investigation [OV-6].
late_effects_screening: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy: Monitor symptoms, provide supportive care [OV-D].
Cardiovascular toxicity: Especially with anthracyclines or bevacizumab; monitor blood pressure, echocardiography as indicated [OV-D].
Secondary malignancies: Long-term risk from platinum or radiation therapy; regular wellness care [OV-6].
Fertility and sexual health: Address in survivorship; refer to REI for fertility preservation options [OV-1].
Psychosocial distress: Screen and manage with supportive care [NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management].
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence can be local (pelvic), peritoneal, or distant. Platinum-sensitive recurrence occurs ≥6 months after prior chemotherapy, while platinum-resistant recurrence occurs <6 months or during therapy [OV-7].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Visits every 2–4 months for 2 years after primary treatment, then every 3–6 months for the next 3 years, and annually thereafter [OV-6].
Physical exam including pelvic exam as clinically indicated at each visit [OV-6].
Tumor markers (e.g., CA-125) if initially elevated, measured at each visit [OV-6].
imaging_strategy: Chest/abdomen/pelvis (C/A/P) CT scan as clinically indicated for symptoms or rising CA-125 [OV-A, Table 2].
PET/CT or MRI for indeterminate findings or high-risk recurrence [OV-A].
For low-grade serous carcinoma, surveillance may include more frequent imaging due to indolent course [LCOC-8].
For malignant germ cell tumors, surveillance includes C/A/P CT and tumor markers per schedule [LCOC-B].
laboratory_monitoring: CBC and chemistry profile as indicated during chemotherapy and follow-up [OV-4, OV-6].
CA-125 or other tumor markers (e.g., inhibin for granulosa cell tumors) if elevated at diagnosis [OV-1, LCOC-12].
Genetic testing results (BRCA, HRD) to inform maintenance therapy decisions [OV-C].
supportive_follow_up: Long-term wellness care including cancer screening for secondary malignancies [OV-6, NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship].
Symptom management for chronic toxicities (e.g., neuropathy, fatigue) [OV-D].

special_situations.key_message: Consider for patients with early-stage disease or low-risk tumors who desire future fertility.
special_situations.management: Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (USO) with comprehensive staging for stage IA unilateral tumors; bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) with uterine preservation for bilateral disease [OV-1, OV-B].
special_situations.evidence: Fertility-sparing surgery does not compromise survival in selected patients [76-84].

special_situations.situation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS)
special_situations.key_message: For patients with advanced-stage disease who are poor surgical candidates or have low likelihood of optimal cytoreduction.
special_situations.management: 3-4 cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy, followed by IDS with completion hysterectomy/BSO and cytoreduction; consider HIPEC for stage III disease [OV-2, OV-B].
special_situations.evidence: Non-inferior to primary debulking surgery in selected patients; improves surgical outcomes [122-125].

special_situations.key_message: May be considered for stage III or IV patients with optimal cytoreduction after neoadjuvant therapy.
special_situations.management: Cisplatin 100 mg/m2 HIPEC during IDS; sodium thiosulfate for renal protection [OV-2, OV-B].
special_situations.evidence: Improves relapse-free survival and overall survival in some studies [129-131].

special_situations.situation: Elderly patients (≥70 years) or with comorbidities
special_situations.key_message: Tolerability to combination chemotherapy may be reduced; adjust dosing accordingly.
special_situations.management: Consider dose-reduced regimens (e.g., carboplatin AUC 5 with paclitaxel 135 mg/m2) or weekly schedules; assess geriatric status [OV-D, 7 of 12].
special_situations.evidence: Alternate dosing improves completion rates and reduces toxicity [178,183-185].

special_situations.key_message: Maintenance therapy with PARP inhibitors significantly improves outcomes.
special_situations.management: After first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, offer olaparib, niraparib, or combination with bevacizumab based on BRCA/HRD status and prior bevacizumab use [OV-5].
special_situations.evidence: SOLO-1, PAOLA-1, PRIMA trials demonstrate PFS and OS benefits [207-214].

special_situations.key_message: Platinum rechallenge is generally not recommended; non-platinum-based regimens preferred.
special_situations.management: Options include weekly paclitaxel, liposomal doxorubicin, gemcitabine, with or without bevacizumab; targeted therapies based on biomarkers (e.g., mirvetuximab for FRα-high) [OV-D, 9 of 12].
special_situations.evidence: AURELIA trial shows improved PFS with bevacizumab combination [257]; FORWARD I trial for mirvetuximab [274].

special_situations.key_message: Management varies by histology; clinical trials preferred due to limited data.
special_situations.management: e.g., For low-grade serous carcinoma, hormone therapy and MEK inhibitors; for germ cell tumors, BEP chemotherapy [LCOC-7, LCOC-14].
special_situations.evidence: Trametinib for low-grade serous [349]; BEP for germ cell tumors [379-381].

clinical_pearls: All patients with suspected ovarian cancer should be referred to a gynecologic oncologist for optimal surgical management, as this is associated with improved survival [51,85,86].
Comprehensive surgical staging is critical for accurate prognosis and treatment planning, as upstaging occurs in ~30% of patients [66-69].
Germline and somatic BRCA testing should be performed early to guide maintenance therapy with PARP inhibitors [OV-C].
Bevacizumab-containing regimens should be withheld 4-6 weeks prior to surgery due to impaired healing [OV-D, 2 of 12].
Platinum hypersensitivity reactions increase with re-exposure; consider desensitization protocols or switch to non-platinum agents [OV-E].
For mucinous ovarian carcinoma, rule out GI primary with evaluation, as treatment may differ [LCOC-4].
Low-grade serous carcinoma is often chemoresistant; hormone therapy and MEK inhibitors are preferred options [LCOC-7, LCOC-8].
In platinum-resistant disease, mirror tumor biomarker testing for targeted therapies, including MSI, TMB, and NTRK fusions [OV-C, OV-D].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Ovarian Cancer Including Fallopian Tube Cancer and Primary Peritoneal Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 4.2026, updated April 10, 2026. Includes algorithms, principles of surgery, pathology, systemic therapy, and management of drug reactions.

guidelines_resources.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Ovarian Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for surveillance and CA-125 monitoring [OV-6].

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens from Patients with Primary Tumors of the Ovary, Fallopian Tube, or Peritoneum
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides standardized reporting for ovarian cancer specimens [OV-C].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
guidelines_resources.notes: Details genetic testing and risk-reducing strategies [OV-1].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care
guidelines_resources.notes: Includes supportive care and symptom management [OV-6].

key_trials.full_name: Olaparib in Patients with BRCA1 or BRCA2 Mutated Advanced Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer and germline BRCA1/2 mutations, in complete or partial response after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy.
key_trials.key_result: PFS significantly improved with olaparib vs placebo (median not reached vs 13.8 months; HR 0.30; 95% CI 0.22-0.41; P<0.001).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: At 7-year follow-up, OS rate 67% vs 46% (HR 0.55; 95% CI 0.36-0.84) [211].
key_trials.practice_change: Established maintenance olaparib as standard for BRCA-mutated advanced ovarian cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Olaparib Plus Bevacizumab as First-Line Maintenance in Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer, regardless of BRCA status, who had a response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy plus bevacizumab.
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved with olaparib/bevacizumab in HRD-positive population (median 37.2 vs 17.7 months; HR 0.33; 95% CI 0.25-0.45; P<0.001). No significant OS benefit in ITT population.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS in HRD subgroup: HR 0.62; 95% CI 0.45-0.85 [212].
key_trials.practice_change: Supported olaparib/bevacizumab maintenance for HRD-positive patients.

key_trials.full_name: Niraparib Maintenance in Patients with Newly Diagnosed Advanced Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer in complete or partial response after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy.
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved in HRD-positive population (median 21.9 vs 10.4 months; HR 0.43; P<0.001); ITT population (13.8 vs 8.2 months; HR 0.62; P<0.001) [209].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Final OS analysis showed no significant difference [213].
key_trials.practice_change: Established niraparib as maintenance option regardless of HRD status.

key_trials.full_name: A Phase III Trial of Bevacizumab in Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer.
key_trials.intervention: Paclitaxel/carboplatin plus bevacizumab throughout, or with bevacizumab only during chemotherapy, or chemotherapy alone.
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved with bevacizumab throughout vs chemotherapy alone (14.1 vs 10.3 months; HR 0.63; P<0.001) [202].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No OS benefit in final analysis [320].
key_trials.practice_change: Supported use of bevacizumab in first-line setting for advanced ovarian cancer.

key_trials.full_name: A Randomised Trial in Ovarian Cancer of Bevacizumab and Chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Patients with newly diagnosed ovarian cancer, mostly stage II-IV.
key_trials.intervention: Paclitaxel/carboplatin plus bevacizumab, followed by bevacizumab maintenance
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant OS benefit in overall population [204].
key_trials.practice_change: Confirmed bevacizumab benefit in high-risk patients.

key_trials.full_name: Bevacizumab Combined with Chemotherapy for Platinum-Resistant Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer.
key_trials.intervention: Chemotherapy (paclitaxel, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or topotecan) plus bevacizumab
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved with bevacizumab combination (6.7 vs 3.4 months; HR 0.48; P<0.001) [257].

key_trials.full_name: Mirvetuximab Soravtansine vs Chemotherapy in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with platinum-resistant ovarian cancer and high FRα expression (≥50% tumor cells).
key_trials.key_result: No significant PFS difference in ITT population; in high FRα subgroup, PFS improved (5.6 vs 3.9 months; HR 0.66) [273].
key_trials.practice_change: Led to accelerated approval for high FRα expression.

key_trials.full_name: Mirvetuximab Soravtansine in FRα-Positive Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with FRα-high platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (≥75% tumor cells with moderate/strong staining).

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab vs Placebo Plus Weekly Paclitaxel ± Bevacizumab in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer, PD-L1 CPS ≥1.
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved in PD-L1 CPS ≥1 population (8.3 vs 7.2 months; HR 0.65; P=0.0014) [285].
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab combination for PD-L1-positive platinum-resistant disease.
